title: Calling Roll — Your First Loop
instruction:
In Chapter 4, you checked ONE student's score. But Riverside Academy has 30 students — checking each one manually would take forever. That's exactly what loops are for.

Go has a single loop keyword: `for`. Write a loop that prints the numbers `1` through `5`, each on its own line — like a teacher calling out seat numbers.
hint: for i := 1; i <= 5; i++ { fmt.Println(i) } — i starts at 1, runs while i <= 5, increments by 1 each round.

title: Loop Through Scores
instruction:
The class just got their exam results back. You have five scores stored one by one. Loop through them and print each score.

The scores are already declared — loop from `i = 0` to `i < 5` and use `fmt.Println(scores[i])`.

*(We'll learn about slices in Chapter 7 — for now, think of `scores` as a list of 5 values.)*
hint: for i := 0; i < 5; i++ { fmt.Println(scores[i]) } — indices go from 0 to 4.

title: Calculate the Class Average
instruction:
The principal wants to know the class average. Use a loop to **sum** all the scores, then divide by the number of students.

You already know how to loop through scores (previous step!). Now add each score to a running `total`, then calculate the average.
hint: Start with total := 0. Inside the loop: total += scores[i]. After the loop: average := total / len(scores).

title: Count Passing Students
instruction:
How many students passed? Use the grade rules from Chapter 4: a score of `60` or higher is a pass.

Combine a loop with the `if` statement you learned in Chapter 4 — increment a counter every time a score is 60 or above.
hint: Declare passes := 0. Inside the loop: if scores[i] >= 60 { passes++ }. Print passes after the loop.

title: Find the Top Score
instruction:
Who is the top student? Loop through the scores and track the highest one you've seen so far.

Start by assuming the first score is the highest. Then compare every other score against your current `highest` — if a score is bigger, update `highest`.
hint: highest := scores[0]. Then loop from i=1 and: if scores[i] > highest { highest = scores[i] }.

title: The Waiting List — while-Style Loop
instruction:
Go's `for` can also work like a while loop — just omit the init and post parts: `for condition { }`.

The waiting list has `12` students. Every week, 3 students get admitted. Print how many weeks it takes to clear the whole list.

Loop while `waitingList > 0`, subtracting 3 each week and counting weeks.
hint: for waitingList > 0 { waitingList -= 3; weeks++ } — then print weeks.

title: Skip the Absent Students — continue
instruction:
`continue` skips the rest of the current loop iteration and jumps straight to the next one. A score of `-1` means the student was absent — skip those.

Loop through `scores` and print each score, but skip any that equal `-1`.
hint: if scores[i] == -1 { continue } — place this at the top of your loop body.

title: Stop at the First Perfect Score — break
instruction:
`break` exits the loop immediately. The principal wants to know the **position** of the first student who scored `100` (perfect score).

Loop through `scores`. When you find a score of `100`, print `"Perfect score at position"` followed by the index, then break.
hint: if scores[i] == 100 { fmt.Println("Perfect score at position", i); break }
